sources.label: Princeton Magazine, Vol. 6 (1891), pp. 428-9 — biographical sketch

institutions.institution_blurb: Class of 1869, A.M. 1872. Class Secretary and Historian; American Whig Society; Iota Chapter of Delta Psi.

education.notes: Preliminary training; later attended the University of Rochester in his Freshman year before transferring to Princeton.
Princeton class of 1869; member of the American Whig Society; Iota Chapter of Delta Psi (joined at Rochester); elected Secretary and Historian of his class.
Prepared for the ministry of the Presbyterian Church.

career.notes: First and only pastoral charge — twenty-five years. Twice Moderator of the Presbytery of Rochester; editor of the Presbyterian Church Herald; member of the Livingston County Historical Society.

career.organization: Association of Descendants of Andrew Ward
career.notes: Compiler of the Andrew Ward Family Genealogy.

Published two volumes of class history: 'After Nine Years' (1878) and 'The Vigintennial Volume of 1869' (1889) — a finely illustrated twenty-year record of the Princeton class. Also published several religious pamphlets including 'The Strength of Young Manhood' and 'The Memorial of Elizabeth Dippy'.

narrative_blocks.header: Switched colleges mid-freshman year for Princeton
narrative_blocks.body: George began his college career at the University of Rochester but, midway through his Freshman year, transferred to Princeton — bringing his Iota Chapter of Delta Psi membership with him. He graduated as Old Nassau's Class Secretary and Historian in 1869, then earned his A.M. in 1872 and finished at Princeton Theological Seminary the next year.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five-year pastorate at Dansville Presbyterian
narrative_blocks.body: After ordination George took the pastorate at Dansville Presbyterian Church in upstate New York and stayed for a quarter century — from 1873 until 1898. Twice elected Moderator of the Presbytery of Rochester, he edited the Presbyterian Church Herald and sat on the Livingston County Historical Society alongside his pastoral work.

narrative_blocks.header: Compiled the Andrew Ward Family Genealogy
narrative_blocks.body: As Secretary of the Association of Descendants of Andrew Ward, George compiled the family genealogy that traces the Ward line back to East Haddam Connecticut. He also published two Princeton class histories — 'After Nine Years' (1878) and 'The Vigintennial Volume of 1869' (1889) — the latter a finely illustrated twenty-year reunion record.

narrative_blocks.header: Father was former Mayor of Rochester New York
narrative_blocks.body: George was the son of Hon. Levi A. Ward, a former Mayor of Rochester, and the grandson of Dr. Levi Ward, a pioneer of Western New York who had emigrated from East Haddam Connecticut early in the 19th century. He was also a cousin of Prof. Henry A.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin of the Rochester natural-science magnate
narrative_blocks.body: George was a first cousin of Prof. Henry Augustus Ward, the naturalist who founded Ward's Natural Science Establishment in Rochester. The firm supplied geological, zoological, and anthropological specimens to universities and museums across the United States — and the cousins shared the Rochester Ward extended family's scholarly orientation, George turning to ministry and genealogy where Henry turned to natural history.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandfather pioneered Western New York from Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: George's grandfather Dr. Levi Ward emigrated from East Haddam Connecticut early in the 19th century and was among the pioneer settlers of Western New York, establishing the Ward family in Rochester. The migration west from a Connecticut town that itself had been a 17th-century Puritan settlement carried the family's New England Calvinist culture into the Genesee Valley.

cross_connections.display_label: Granddaughter Carolyn Pierpont Ward carries her name forward two ways

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter's marriage produced a Hooker cousin convergence
narrative_blocks.body: Caroline married Rev. George Kemp Ward in 1873 and bore four children — Levi, Ruth, Charles, and Kenneth — all Hooker descendants through her Pierpont line. When her daughter Ruth married Horace Willard Hooker in 1906, the union produced the project's third documented Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage, with both spouses tracing descent to Rev. Thomas Hooker through different branches.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Caroline Editha Pierpont Ward (HD1545). Edwards's middle name 'Pierpont' carries her maiden surname forward — distinctive among his four siblings, none of whom received the Pierpont middle name. See naming_inspiration.

narrative_blocks.header: Only sibling carrying mother's maiden surname Pierpont
narrative_blocks.body: Of George Kemp Ward and Caroline Editha Pierpont's five children, only Edwards — the eldest — received his mother's maiden surname Pierpont as his middle name. His daughter Carolyn Pierpont Ward (b.1914) carried the pattern forward to a third generation, and Carolyn's first name Carolyn itself echoes her grandmother Caroline.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his mother's maiden surname Pierpont forward — and through her the broader Pierpont line back to Mary Hooker Pierpont and Thomas Hooker. Of George + Caroline's five children, only Edwards (the eldest) carries this middle name.

birth.note: Pennsylvania per 1910 census derivative; needs precise location.

death.note: Marked 'Deceased' on FamilySearch without exact date.
Marked 'Deceased' on FamilySearch without exact date.
Marked 'Deceased' on FamilySearch without exact date.

cross_connections.display_label: First name and middle name both honor her grandmother

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries her grandmother Caroline Editha Pierpont's maiden surname forward — the same Pierpont middle name her father Edwards (HD3407) carried, making three generations of Pierpont as middle name in the line.
First name Carolyn echoes her grandmother Caroline's first name — the same grandmother whose Pierpont surname she also carries as her middle name.
First name Marguerite echoes her mother Mary Marguerite Whipple Ward's middle name.
Middle name carries her mother's maiden surname Whipple forward.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY. FindAGrave 72197309.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Caroline Editha Pierpont Ward (HD1545); Hooker descent runs through her Pierpont line. Levi carries his paternal grandfather's name (Hon. Levi A. Ward, Mayor of Rochester) — see naming_inspiration.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for his mayoral Rochester grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Dansville while his father Rev. George Kemp Ward held the Presbyterian pastorate there, Levi carried his paternal grandfather's name forward. Hon. Levi A. Ward had served as Mayor of Rochester earlier in the 19th century — making the eldest son's first and middle names a two-generation civic legacy bound up in the Rochester Ward family.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his paternal grandfather Hon. Levi A. Ward, who served as Mayor of Rochester NY in the mid-19th century. Hon. Levi A. Ward is not built into the JSON tree — he is the father of George Kemp Ward (X01665), Caroline Editha Pierpont's husband, and not himself a Hooker descendant.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY. FindAGrave 72197439.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his mother's maiden surname Bishop forward. Julia Pancost Bishop Ward's family was from Rochester NY.

burial.plot_notes: Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester NY. FindAGrave 26515170.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Caroline Editha Pierpont Ward (HD1545, 1852-1939). Through her mother Ruth descends from Thomas Hooker via the Pierpont line — making her 1906 marriage to Horace Willard Hooker (H03437) a cousin marriage of two Hooker descendants.

cross_connections.display_label: Married a Hooker cousin descended through the Horace James line

careers.notes: Per Wikipedia: 'Willard Hooker had held roles such as vice president and treasurer' in Hooker Electrochemical. Likely refers to Horace Willard. Fourth engineer-brother in this Rochester generation.

notable.notable_blurb: Married his Pierpont-line Hooker cousin Ruth Ward

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Hooker cousin via Pierpont line
narrative_blocks.body: On 31 May 1906 Horace married Ruth Ward, daughter of Rev. George Kemp Ward and Caroline Editha Pierpont. Through her mother Ruth was herself a Hooker descendant via the Pierpont line — making the marriage one of the project's documented cousin marriages, with both spouses tracing descent to Rev. Thomas Hooker through different branches.

narrative_blocks.header: Treasurer of a New York investment firm
narrative_blocks.body: Per Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy, Horace was treasurer of the Development and Funding Co. in New York City. He continued the engineering and finance patterns of his immediate Hooker forebears — his father Horace B. the Civil War engineer, and his older brother Elon Huntington Hooker the Hooker Electrochemical founder.

cross_connections.display_label: Married a Hooker cousin descended through the Pierpont line

career.notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909: treasurer of the Development and Funding Co. of New York City.

parents.father_research_notes: Father Horace Willard Hooker Sr. (H03437) descends from Thomas Hooker via the Horace James Hooker line.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother Ruth Ward Hooker (HD3400) descends from Thomas Hooker via her mother Caroline Editha Pierpont (HD1545) and the Pierpont line. As both parents are Hooker descendants, Horace Jr. is a dual-line descendant.
Mother Ruth Ward Hooker (HD3400). Middle name 'Ward' carries his maternal grandfather Rev. George Kemp Ward (X01665) and his mother's maiden line.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his maternal grandfather Rev. George Kemp Ward and his mother's maiden surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Twice married, one child by each wife
narrative_blocks.body: Charles wed Nellie May Wyness in 1906 and they had a son Louis Alexander; after their divorce Charles married Rose Marguerita Boothe of Groveland and they had a daughter Mildred. Both Hooker-line children carry the Ward surname forward; both wives outlived him.

marriages.notes: Second marriage to a Hoppough — given name not on record. She carried this final married surname at death.

sources.label: FindAGrave — Nellie May Wyness Ward Hoppough

burial.plot_notes: Lakeview Cemetery, Brockport NY. FindAGrave 250562693. Note: distinct from his father's Lakeview Groveland burial.
Lakeview Cemetery, Groveland NY. FindAGrave per family records.

marriages.notes: Second marriage to a Makely after Harold Booth's 1954 death; carried this surname at death.

birth.note: City inferred — his father George was pastor at Dansville 1873-1898.

burial.plot_notes: Ferncliff Cemetery and Mausoleum, Hartsdale NY.

parents.mother_research_notes: Hooker descent via Pierpont line. Middle name 'Kemp' carries his father's middle name forward.

sources.label: New York Times — Kenneth Kemp Ward obituary, April 1939

narrative_blocks.header: Wartime France service then Wall Street partner
narrative_blocks.body: Kenneth shipped to France with the American Expeditionary Forces in WWI, then returned to Manhattan in 1919 and joined Hemphill Noyes & Co. — the investment banking partnership at 15 Broad Street that he served as partner for the rest of his life. The arc from young Guaranty Trust clerk through the trenches to Wall Street partnership took just over five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Held the 1921 St. Andrew's Golf Club championship
narrative_blocks.body: Kenneth held the club championship at St. Andrew's Golf Club in Hastings-on-Hudson NY in 1921, three years after returning from France. He chaired the club's golf committee from 1922 to 1923 and remained on the board of governors as a 25-year member of the club — one of the country's oldest, founded 1888.

narrative_blocks.header: Heart attack at Shelter Island at fifty-one
narrative_blocks.body: Kenneth died suddenly of a heart attack on Shelter Island NY on 2 April 1939, age 51, just over three years into his marriage to Florence McAndrews. He left no children. Buried Ferncliff Cemetery and Mausoleum in Hartsdale NY, away from his Rochester family.

cross_connections.display_label: a fellow Guaranty Trust executive and wartime veteran

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name carries his father George Kemp Ward's middle name forward to the next generation.

career.organization: Guaranty Trust Company of New York

career.notes: Joined the investment banking firm at 15 Broad Street upon return from France in 1919; remained partner until his 1939 death.

military_service.notes: Served overseas in France during the First World War, after early career employment at Guaranty Trust Company of New York. Returned 1919 and joined Hemphill, Noyes & Co.

burialCemetery.name: LittleVille Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Avon NY cemetery containing the Pierrepont-Ward family burials of the Rochester branch (Jonathan Edwards Pierrepont HD1092, his daughter Caroline Editha Pierpont Ward HD1545, and her husband Rev. George Kemp Ward X01665).

INST023.name: Hopkins Academy
INST023.notes: Hadley, Massachusetts academy founded from the bequest of Edward Hopkins — the same bequest behind Hopkins Grammar School, New Haven (INST001), but a DISTINCT institution. Roger Hooker Leavitt and Hart Leavitt are graduates; compiler flagged the distinction Stage 6Y-687.

INST145.name: Princeton Theological Seminary
INST145.notes:
Founded 1812 Princeton NJ by the General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church. The largest theological seminary in the Presbyterian Church (USA). Separate from Princeton University (College of New Jersey, INST023) though closely associated in its early decades.

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 4 in the seminary's first century:

EARLY 19TH CENTURY:
- TD0255 Rev. Joseph Huntington Jones DD (1797-1868, Talcott gen 7; attended Princeton Theological Seminary after Harvard A.B. 1817; subsequently Pastor of Sixth Presbyterian Church Philadelphia for 23 years 1838-1861; 1843 honorary DD from Lafayette College; author of 6 theological works 1839-1864)
- TD0238 Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones (1774-1843, mother of TD0255; documented in research notes as supporting her son's seminary education)

MID-19TH CENTURY:
- HD1973 Theodore Dwight Woolsey (1801-1889; Yale Divinity + Princeton Theological Seminary background before Yale presidency)
- X01665 Rev. George Kemp Ward (later 19th century clergyman)

Princeton Theological's role as the principal Old School Presbyterian institution made it the natural destination for TD0255 Joseph H. Jones — whose entire Philadelphia pastoral career followed Old School Presbyterian theology.
